questions.question: 中文导读：A file on disk is decoded into a NumPy array with shape (224, 224, 3) and dtype uint8. What does each number represent?
options: 中文选项：The probability that a pixel is that colour, between 0 and 1
中文选项：One photon count per detector, normalized to the full dynamic range of the sensor
中文选项：A sample of light intensity at one grid position, quantized to one of 256 levels per channel
中文选项：A floating-point brightness value ready to feed to a convolutional network
questions.explanation: 中文解析：A uint8 image stores 8-bit integers in [0, 255]. Each integer is one quantized sample of light intensity at one (row, column, channel) position. It is neither a probability nor a float; you must divide by 255 and standardize before feeding it to a pretrained model.

questions.question: 中文导读：You load an image with Pillow and get an array of shape (480, 640, 3). You pass it as a batched tensor (1, 480, 640, 3) to a PyTorch Conv2d with in_channels=3. What happens?
options: 中文选项：Nothing — PyTorch auto-detects the layout
中文选项：The first conv layer treats height as the channel axis, producing meaningless feature maps
中文选项：PyTorch raises a RuntimeError: Conv2d expects NCHW, sees 480 channels where the weight expects 3, and refuses to run
中文选项：Accuracy drops by a few percent but inference still works
questions.explanation: 中文解析：PyTorch Conv2d enforces strict channel-count checking against the weight tensor. A batched HWC input (1, 480, 640, 3) is interpreted as NCHW with C=480, which does not match the weight's expected 3 channels, and PyTorch raises RuntimeError before any computation. The loud failure is a feature — it forces you to fix the layout. You must permute to NCHW (`.permute(0, 3, 1, 2)`) before feeding PyTorch.

questions.question: 中文导读：Why do ImageNet pretrained models expect inputs standardized with mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406] and std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]?
options: 中文选项：Those are the RGB values of an average natural scene
中文选项：Those are the per-channel mean and standard deviation of the ImageNet training set in [0, 1] space, so subtracting them centers the distribution the model was trained on
中文选项：They make the input strictly zero-mean unit-variance for any image
中文选项：They are required by the ReLU activation function
questions.explanation: 中文解析：The numbers are statistics of the ImageNet training corpus computed after dividing pixels by 255. Using them aligns your input distribution with the one the network saw during training. For a model trained on a different dataset you would recompute the stats on that dataset.

questions.question: 中文导读：You resize a segmentation mask (integer class IDs 0..20) from 500x500 to 224x224. Which interpolation method is correct?
options: 中文选项：Bilinear — it smooths the class boundaries
中文选项：Bicubic — it preserves sharpness
中文选项：Lanczos — highest quality
中文选项：Nearest neighbour — it preserves valid integer class IDs instead of inventing fractional ones
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Bilinear, bicubic, and lanczos average neighbouring values. On a class-ID mask that produces non-integer values like 4.7 between class 4 and class 5, which are not real classes. Nearest neighbour picks the closest original pixel and keeps the label space intact. This rule also applies to any channel that encodes IDs or indices.

questions.question: 中文导读：RGB grayscale conversion uses weights 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B rather than 0.333 R + 0.333 G + 0.333 B. Why?
options: 中文选项：To compensate for JPEG compression artifacts in each channel
中文选项：Because green photons carry more energy than red or blue photons
中文选项：Because human vision is most sensitive to green and least sensitive to blue, so the weighted sum matches perceived luminance
中文选项：To match the behaviour of the Bayer filter on camera sensors
questions.explanation: 中文解析：The ITU-R BT.601 weights 0.299/0.587/0.114 come from the luminous efficiency of the human eye. An equal-weight average would make green-tinted images look too dark and red ones too bright to a human observer. Most classical computer-vision grayscale code uses these weights; BT.709 (0.2126/0.7152/0.0722) is the modern HDTV variant for linear-light inputs.
